item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it is clearly explained now that animals do atack any species of the kingdom Animalia to use anything they have as flesh.
I like to imagine a horse skeleton surrounded by worm, insect, arachnid, bird or any kind of tissue it was able to find and that that is how this bog became known to the Foundation or something similar to this. Perhaps I wish the skeletons didn't just use mammals as their source in the experiments since I feel there are tons of ideas left to the imagination in there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was about to downvote for inconsistencies (whether animals only attack their own species or not) and lack of any hook…but the interview and exploration log…i liked them +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad at all, though I do think you could explore the whole "3106-A isn't very hostile" thing. Maybe you could do something with a domesticated animal entering, and then coming out without being overly hostile, but begging. I just feel that you could do more with the concept of some things entering not being very hostile upon exiting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The last two lines of conversation in the Exploration Log just make this one for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cleaned tags. 'Bog' is unrecognized and '3000' is reserved for articles submitted for the SCP-3000 contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: please remove the tags, the tag team will take care of them for you.

item.title: Well done!
item.content:encoded: While it's pretty obvious that this is your first SCP, it's very well done for what it is. Like others said, work on cohesion and dialogue. Otherwise, it stands pretty well on its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a really cool idea, and it has elements that really worked. I love the descriptions of how the -A feels with and without the meat, and the imagery is great. With that said, there are issues:

  • The backstory about the kid is kind of awkward and not super impactful
  • The -A's dialogue could use work. Good dialogue is really hard to write, and it's definitely better here than I usually see, but I still found it a little distracting. When in doubt, less is more.
  • The experiment log doesn't add a ton. You could summarize the findings from it in a sentence in the description.
  • The SCP as a whole lacks cohesion. There are a bunch of cool elements, but they don't tie together super well.
  • There's a number of typos and incorrect words in places.

Overall I think this has potential, but it's just not quite there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you enjoyed it! And I appreciate the advice. My experimentation log actually used to be more than twice as long, but a lot of people felt how you did, so I cut most of it out.

I'll keep in mind to make the description a little more interesting. I'm still reading a lot of articles to learn how to paint a compelling image while using a clinical tone and saying no more than needs to be said.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the review. I'm trying to learn as much as I can from more experienced members so feedback is always appreciated, and I'll do my best to keep it in mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's interesting to see this at exactly 0 with so many votes. I liked it — enough to +1 — but I feel like it dragged a bit. The parts about the skeleton-zombies at the beginning were the least interesting, and I feel like some of that and the experiment log with it could be cut to get to the D-class character, which was the part I liked ("Do you feel weird talking to a skeleton?"). I enjoyed the dreamlike ambiguity of the entire thing from there; questions like "how is he talking with no lungs" didn't even occur to me, because I feel like this is all about adapting to situations that have gone sideways from the expected.

I can see what A Random Day means about spreading out the exposition, though I'm not really sure how you would do that. As someone who enjoyed this, though, I what it needs most is to be streamlined a bit to do what it's doing even better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's potential here. There's an interesting explanation and relatively compelling rationale for why the things that come out of the bog try to kill. Unfortunately, the method by which that explanation is provided suffers. Aside from the question of how a skeleton can talk with no lungs or larynx, the D-Class' character is used exclusively as a vehicle to provide exposition.

What the bog is, what it does, what it's like being in the bog, why the animals do what they do, and why the bog does what it does - essentially everything interesting about the article - these are all provided in a single easy-to-digest format through the interview, which isn't rewarding for the reader. Furthermore, it can't act as payoff since it's in the middle of the article and there's very little buildup or story progression before the interview. The exploration log starts relatively strong, but then falls into the same trap of exposition. There's potential for character development through the relationship between the D-Class and the bog, but it's not played to its full strengths.

item.title: Re: not a staff post
item.content:encoded: Sorry about that. I accidentally saved the draft while editing and I wasn't sure what to do so I just deleted the draft text and continued editing. Then I posted as soon as all my XXXX's were 3106's.

item.title: not a staff post
item.content:encoded: No need to delete as the author has put the text back in the page
